html:
Strix Group (SG9.F / KETL): post-Billi, the market is still pricing the old balance sheet

Strix is no longer the same leveraged, messy kettle-controls roll-up that screens badly on stale data. The central change is the January 2026 completion of the Billi disposal, which brought in about £105 million net proceeds and left the group with roughly £35 million of net cash at completion. That sale was not a retreat at a loss. Strix bought Billi for about £38 million in 2022 and sold it for £110 million enterprise value, crystallizing roughly 3x its original investment.

The market still seems to be treating Strix as a pressured consumer-appliance supplier with debt risk. That was a reasonable fear in 2024 and 2025. It is much less reasonable now. Since the disposal, Strix has returned £10 million through a fully taken-up tender at 43p and had already bought back 8.36 million shares at an average 41.2p under its £10 million buyback program. Total voting rights are now 198.3 million shares. At the current SG9.F quote around €0.47, that is roughly €93 million of equity value. After the tender and buyback spend, pro forma net cash is still likely around £20 million before further trading movements, putting enterprise value near £60 million.

That is the setup: a globally dominant safety-control franchise, no longer balance-sheet constrained, trading at perhaps 4-5x normalized forward free cash flow if the retained business can simply recover to mid-cycle.

The May 2026 trading update is not explosive, but it is exactly the kind of early evidence the thesis needs. For the 15-month FY26 period, Strix reiterated about £150 million of revenue and £9.8-10.2 million adjusted PBT, while noting that Controls volumes outside domestic China were consistently ahead of the comparable 2025 period. More important than the headline PBT, which is distorted by the 15-month period and Billi transition, is the direction: low-cost and next-generation controls are gaining OEM interest, Consumer Goods has returned to growth, and management is pushing surcharges to offset copper and silver inflation.

The variant view is that investors are looking at a sold-off legacy kettle supplier, while the real asset is a safety-critical component monopoly-ish business with unusually sticky OEM relationships. The 2024 annual report still shows Controls at about £69.5 million of revenue and Consumer Goods at about £32 million, before the Billi sale. Controls is the crown jewel: Strix has roughly half the global kettle-controls market by value, long customer qualification cycles, product-liability importance, regulatory know-how, and IP enforcement history. The new low-cost control matters because it lets Strix attack less regulated markets where copyist manufacturers have pressured share, even if the initial margin mix is lower. The next-generation control matters because OEM design-ins can lock in product cycles for years.

The retained Consumer Goods piece is lower quality than Controls, but it is not worthless filler. After SKU rationalization, the division is being aimed at water filtration, Aqua Optima and LAICA, OEM solutions, and contaminant-reduction technology such as PFAS filtration. That gives Strix a second route to consumables revenue rather than only appliance-cycle hardware.

The stock is cheap even after the move. Using the current Frankfurt quote around €0.47, the equity is about £80-85 million. If the retained group can do £105-115 million of annual revenue, £20-23 million of EBITDA, and £12-15 million of free cash flow after tax and maintenance capex, the stock is on roughly 6x equity free cash flow and about 4x enterprise free cash flow. That is too low for a net-cash, cash-returning, IP-backed industrial component franchise, even allowing for small-cap illiquidity and consumer-appliance cyclicality.

This is also not a lazy “net cash makes EV look cheap” story. Cash is already being returned. The tender and buyback are concrete evidence that the Billi proceeds are not just being trapped on the balance sheet. The remaining test is whether the incoming CEO keeps capital allocation disciplined rather than using the restored balance sheet for another acquisition before the core recovery is fully visible.

The main bear case is margin mix. If recovery is mainly in low-cost controls and regulated-market volumes stay weak, Strix can grow units without restoring historical profitability. Copper, silver, plastics and tariff volatility can also eat the benefit if surcharges lag. CEO transition is a real risk, but also an opportunity: Mark Bartlett steps down at the end of May 2026, and a new CEO inherits a net-cash balance sheet rather than a refinancing problem.

Retail and market attention remain thin. The Frankfurt line is extremely illiquid, UK retail discussion is sparse and backward-looking, and the latest Investors Chronicle stance was only “Hold” despite the Billi disposal and capital return. That is useful. The stock is not being chased as a fashionable turnaround. It is still being priced like a damaged AIM small-cap.

Targets

Bear: €0.30, about 36% downside from €0.47. Regulated-market demand stays weak, low-cost controls dilute margins, Consumer Goods growth fades, and normalized free cash flow is only £6-7 million. A 6x FCF valuation plus residual net cash supports roughly 25-27p, or about €0.30.

Base: €0.95, about 102% upside. Controls volumes recover modestly, surcharges hold margins, Consumer Goods grows low single digits, and the retained group reaches £13-15 million of free cash flow. A 9-10x FCF multiple plus net cash gives roughly 80-85p, or about €0.95.

Bull: €1.65, about 251% upside. Next-generation and low-cost controls regain share, regulated markets normalize, water filtration becomes a credible growth leg, and buybacks reduce the share count further. At £22-24 million of free cash flow on a 12x multiple, with a still-clean balance sheet, equity value reaches about 140-145p, or roughly €1.65.
